experiences.intentions:
Player 1: No concrete role information exists yet beyond my own being Good. As a Servant on round 1, approving an opening 2-person team that includes myself is standard and helps gather information without risking much; rejecting gives little benefit and can stall. My vote reveals mild confidence in a neutral opening team, which is acceptable for Good.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 1 and 2 are not confirmed from my perspective but this proposed team excludes both evil players, making it likely all-good. As Minion on an early team vote, optimal play is usually to avoid drawing suspicion while trying to prevent a clean good quest from succeeding. Rejecting an all-non-evil team helps evil slow the game and avoids giving good an early success. My single reject does not reveal much on round 1 since early rejections are common.
Player 2: No role information is available yet beyond my own being Good. On round 1, teams are often approved to generate information, and this 2-person team includes me plus the leader. As Good, approving an opening team is standard unless there is prior suspicion, which there is not. My vote reveals a cooperative stance and helps advance the game without giving harmful info.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil, so team [1,2] contains no known evil and is the best possible 2-person team excluding myself. On quest 1 round 1, approving a clean team is optimal for Good. My approve vote is standard and does not reveal special information beyond supporting a strong team.
Player 4: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 1, 2, and 3 are good. The proposed team [1,2] contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and gives good a clean early quest. As evil on round 1, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible to prevent easy good progress and to push for teams containing me or 0 later. My vote itself reveals little because early votes are noisy; rejecting an all-good team helps evil more than approving it.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. Team [1,2] excludes known evil players and also already succeeded on Quest 1 with 0 fails, making it the strongest available 2-person team. On team voting, good should approve clean teams to secure successes; rejecting would only add noise and risk worse future teams. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a highly good-indicative team, which is acceptable here.
Player 1: I am Good with no private info. Team [1,2] succeeded on Quest 1, but Quest 2 with [0,1,2] failed once, so exactly one of {0,1,2} is likely Evil. Since [1,2] had no fail before, this pair is somewhat better than including 0, though it is still possible 2 withheld a fail on Quest 1. As a Servant in team voting, I should favor the most plausible good team available. Approving [1,2] supports a team that excludes the additional suspect 0 and may help Good recover information without wasting votes. My vote reveals some trust in 2, but that is acceptable given current evidence.
Player 2: As a Good Servant, I know quest 1 team [1,2] succeeded, while quest 2 team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail. Since I am good, at least one of 0 or 1 is evil; 2 is me and good. Player 1 now proposes [1,2] again, which excludes 0 and reuses the successful pair from quest 1. That team is plausibly good if 0 was the sole evil on quest 2, but if 1 is evil this is dangerous. Given the evidence, [1,2] is one of the best available 2-person teams and approving helps test whether 1 is trustworthy. My vote reveals moderate trust in 1 based on prior success and the single-fail inference.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate, so 1 and 2 are not confirmed evil from my perspective. Quest 2 with team [0,1,2] failed with exactly one fail, which implicates me or potentially 1/2 to others; since I am evil, excluding me now may make this team look safer to good players. As evil in a team vote, I want to balance appearing consistent with maximizing future control. Approving [1,2] risks a success if both are good, giving Good a 2-1 lead. Rejecting is better because this 2-person team is likely good-heavy and I can also avoid helping a clean-looking team pass.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 2 team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, which could only have come from 0 or me, and I was not on it, so 0 failed there. That makes 1 and 2 somewhat good-leaning from that quest, though 1 could still be Merlin/Percival/Servant. Current phase is team voting on [1,2]. As evil, a 2-player team of two likely-good players is dangerous because it can succeed and put good to match point at 2-1. Rejecting is optimal to try to force inclusion of an evil player later. My vote reveals little since evil commonly rejects all-good teams; with only one evil teammate known, preserving sabotage opportunities matters more than subtlety here.

Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. Proposed team [0,1,4] contains two known evil players, so approving would risk an evil win on quest 4. Optimal good play is to reject. This reveals some suspicion of 0/4, but rejecting such a bad team is necessary and consistent with good play.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate and I am evil. Team [0,1,4] contains both evil players, so if approved we can force a fail on quest 4 and bring evil to 2 points. As Assassin, optimal play is to get this team through; approving is strongly pro-evil. My vote reveals little since many players would plausibly approve their own proposed team, and the strategic upside is high.
Player 2: From the quests: team [1,2] succeeded twice, while [0,1,2] had exactly one fail. Since I am good, that fail came from either 0 or 1. Because 1 was on two clean 2-person teams with me, 1 is very likely good and 0 is the strongest evil suspect. Proposed team [0,1,4] includes suspected evil 0 and excludes confirmed-goodest me/3 info, so as good I should reject to avoid risking quest 4. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 0 without giving evil useful hidden info.
Player 1: We know team [1,2] succeeded twice, so both 1 (me) and 2 are likely good or at least 2 was forced to pass twice; team [0,1,2] had exactly one fail, so the evil is likely either 0 or 2. Since [1,2] succeeded, 2 looks more trustworthy than 0, making 0 the strongest evil suspect. Proposed team [0,1,4] includes suspected 0 and excludes relatively trusted 2. As a good servant in team voting on quest 4, optimal play is to reject teams containing the top suspect when a better alternative exists. My vote reveals I am consistent with suspicion on 0, which helps good more than it hurts.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate. The quest 2 fail came from team [0,1,2], so either I caused it or one of 1/2 is suspicious to others; with [1,2] succeeding twice, 1 and 2 look relatively good, making 0 look suspicious. Current team [0,1,4] includes both evil players, which is excellent for evil because on quest 4 in 5-player Avalon only one fail is needed and we can guarantee a fail if approved. As evil, I should approve any team with strong sabotage potential. My vote supporting this team is consistent with wanting it to pass; revealing that I approve may be somewhat suspicious if the team fails, but securing the chance to fail quest 4 and reach 2-2 is worth it.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,2] includes known Evil player 0, so this team is highly likely to fail and should be rejected. As Good, optimal play is to prevent Evil from getting on quest 4. Rejecting also gives minimal extra information beyond my likely prior skepticism of teams with 0.
Player 2: We know quest 2 team [1,2] had exactly one fail, and I am Good, so Player 1 must be Evil. Quest 1 [0,1,2] succeeded despite including 1, which is possible if 1 chose not to fail. Quest 3 [0,1,4] succeeded with 1 on it, again consistent with 1 being Evil and hiding. For Good, the key is to reject any team containing confirmed Evil 1. Approving would risk an unnecessary fail on quest 4. Rejecting also signals the logical deduction from quest 2.
Player 1: I know I am Good. Team [1,2] produced the only fail, so player 2 is the strongest evil candidate because I know I passed. Teams [0,1] have succeeded twice, making 0 look relatively good; 4 is less cleared. On team voting, optimal good play is to reject teams containing the main suspect 2 when there is a plausible alternative. Rejecting also signals suspicion of 2 without giving evil useful new info beyond my read.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1,2] previously went on quest 2 and produced exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 2 is likely good from others' perspective while 0 is certainly evil to me. On quest 4 with Good leading 2-1, evil wants control of the team or to preserve ambiguity for assassination later. This proposed team includes my teammate 0 and excludes me; since a 3-player quest only needs one fail, approving gives evil a strong chance to tie the game 2-2 if 0 is on it. My vote revealing support for a team with 0 could be mildly suspicious, but as evil I prefer the high-value chance to fail the quest now.
Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 4. Team [0,1,2] contains me and excludes 4, so it has exactly one evil if reads are correct. Prior quest data: [0,1,2] previously failed with 1 fail, strongly implicating me or 2 from Good POV; [1,2] succeeded, which points to 1 and 2 looking good and makes me the likely source of the earlier fail. As evil, on team voting I want to balance getting evil onto quests with avoiding overexposing myself. This team can fail because I am on it, and quest 4 in 5-player Avalon still only needs one fail, so approving gives Evil a strong chance to reach 2 fails. Voting approve is consistent with wanting an evil-containing team to go. My public vote may be somewhat suspicious given history, but the strategic value of an evil-capable team outweighs that.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are Evil, so 1 and 2 must be Good. The proposed team [1,2,3] contains only confirmed Good players and excludes both known Evils. In team voting on quest 4, approving a fully Good team is optimal. My approval signals support for the safest available team and helps Good secure a likely success without exposing extra information beyond normal Merlin-good behavior.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [1,2,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, giving Good a 3rd quest. As evil on quest 4, optimal play is to reject any team without at least one evil player. My reject aligns with preserving evil chances and reveals little since rejecting a 3-good team is plausible for suspicious good players too.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate; 1,2,3 are all nominally good from my information, though one could be Merlin. This is team voting on quest 4, and the proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it likely succeeds and puts Good to 3 successes, which is very bad for evil. My vote itself gives limited info; rejecting is optimal because it may help force a team with an evil player later or create suspicion around these players.
Player 1: I am Good Servant with limited info. Quest 1 succeeded on [1,2], so 1 and 2 looked somewhat good then. Quest 2 failed on [0,1,4], meaning at least one of 0 or 4 is evil since I know I am good. Current proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both 0 and 4, the main suspects. Player 3 is less cleared than 2, but this is still the best-looking 3-person team available from known info. Approving helps Good test a team avoiding the likely evil pair and may secure the third success.
Player 2: I’m Good with limited info. Quest 1 [1,2,3] failed once, so at least one of 1/2/3 is evil; since I know I’m Good, at least one of 1 or 3 is evil. Quest 2 [1,2] succeeded, which slightly clears 1 if standard rules require only one fail, but evil could also choose to pass; still, 3 looks the strongest suspect from these results. Proposed team [1,2,3] reintroduces the likely suspect 3, so as Good I should reject and push for a team excluding 3. My reject signals suspicion of 3 and avoids approving a team that likely contains evil.
